Duckbill Jag-Wing (Anatoserratus alarazor)

a lopsided duck-headed sentinel, half bird half something sharper, marching forward with one saw-wing raised

Specimen

3D model not available.

In motion

Biology

WHAT IT LOOKS LIKE This walking sentinel stands about as tall as a golden retriever, with a body shaped like a tall rectangular robe. Its neck bends sharply forward, like it's always peeking around a corner, ending in a long duck-like snout with a tiny dangling tuft under the chin. One arm is a jagged, saw-toothed wing pointing up and out, while the other side has just a thin stick arm with a small crossbar, like a lowercase "t."

WHAT IT EATS Its mouth is a long, flat duckbill shape with no teeth-type part mentioned in the spec — that's the same setup real filter feeders have. This creature likely sifts small food bits out of mud, water, or soft plants rather than biting or chewing anything tough.

REAL ANIMALS IT'S LIKE - Mallard duck — real ducks have tiny comb-like ridges inside their flat bills called lamellae, which strain tiny bits of food out of muddy water. That's probably how this creature's duckbill works too. - Fiddler crab — fiddler crabs grow one giant claw and one tiny claw, and the big one isn't for eating — it's for waving at other crabs to send a signal. That lopsided body plan matches this creature's one saw-wing, one stick-arm design perfectly.

ITS COOLEST TRICK Just like a fiddler crab's oversized claw, this creature's single jagged sawtooth wing probably isn't built for flying or fighting — it's built for showing off. Raising that one sharp, zigzag wing while walking on two legs would make it look bigger and impossible to miss, a loud visual signal to warn rivals or attract attention, even though the animal never leaves the ground.

Habitat & Ecology

WHERE IT LIVES The Duckbill Jag-Wing calls the Serrated Fens of Krannok Basin home — a soggy, reed-choked marsh laced with black mud and pale, sun-bleached grasses. Cold mist hangs over the water most mornings, and the ground squelches with every step.

ITS NEIGHBORHOOD This is a wetland biome, all shallow pools and spongy peat, perfect for a creature that only walks and never flies. Its thin legs — one a bit shorter than the other — are built for wading through uneven, mucky ground rather than running or swimming fast, so it takes slow, careful, marching steps between reed clumps.

WHO IT SHARES THE FOOD WEB WITH That long, flat duckbill mouth is made for scooping soft marsh plants, roots, and squishy mud-bugs — so the Jag-Wing is a plant-and-bug eater, a primary consumer. Bigger swamp hunters, like the basin's stalking mud-lynx, would happily snap one up if it wandered too far from cover — which is exactly why its one jagged, sawtooth wing matters. When a predator gets close, the Jag-Wing rasps that serrated wing edge against its stiff torso, making a harsh scraping screech that startles hunters into backing off — a trick real insects use, but scaled up into a spooky marsh warning siren.

REAL ANIMALS IT'S LIKE - Platypus — its rubbery duck-like bill can sense the tiny electric signals of prey hiding in murky water, which is exactly the kind of stealthy mud-hunting the Jag-Wing's flat bill hints at. - Hadrosaurs (duck-billed dinosaurs) — these ancient walkers had wide, flat snouts for cropping tough marsh plants and moved in herds across floodplains on sturdy legs, just like a Jag-Wing marching through the fen.

Folklore & Society

SOCIAL STRUCTURE The Duckbill Jag-Wing's stooped, sentinel-like bearing and slow ground-bound gait (locomotion: walk) yield no evidence of flocking behavior; individuals are typically observed alone or in mated pairs holding fixed patrol routes along field edges and hedgerows. Field reports describe the species as territorial in the manner of a lone watchman rather than a herd animal, consistent with its lopsided, "one-armed sentry" vibe.

BREEDING Clutches are small, one to two eggs laid in shallow ground scrapes rather than nests, timed to early spring thaw. Both parents are believed to alternate incubation duty, though only one — typically the wing-bearing individual — remains posted at the scrape entrance during daylight hours, the crossbarred-arm parent foraging in shifts.

LOCAL LORE Rural communities within the species' range refer to it as the "Sawback Warden," a name tied directly to its single serrated wing, which local tradition holds is not plumage at all but a tally of debts owed to the land — one notch cut for every season the creature has stood guard. Farmers hold that disturbing a Warden mid-patrol invites a poor harvest, and it is considered ill luck to point at the raised saw-wing directly. The regional saying, still repeated at planting time, warns: "Point not at the tooth-wing, lest it count you among its debts." No naturalist has traced an anatomical basis for the tally myth, though the asymmetry of the limb pair — one bladed, one a bare crossbar — is frequently cited by locals as proof that the creature was "built to keep score, not to fly."
